general_skills.title: Decompose the claim
general_skills.principle: Break the post into concrete factual claims before judging it.
general_skills.when_to_apply: At the start of every verification episode.

general_skills.title: Cross-check modalities
general_skills.principle: Compare post text, transcript, OCR, and attached frames for consistency.
general_skills.when_to_apply: When a post includes both text and video inputs.

general_skills.title: Judge the post impression
general_skills.principle: Base the verdict on whether the post creates a misleading factual impression, not on isolated suspicious words alone.

general_skills.title: Avoid metadata shortcuts
general_skills.principle: Do not rely on hidden task labels, profile vibes, or source metadata shortcuts when deciding fake versus real.
general_skills.when_to_apply: Whenever extra metadata could bias the judgment.

general_skills.title: Missing proof is not falsity
general_skills.principle: Do not label a claim false solely because the provided post package does not fully prove it. Distinguish weak in-package evidence from evidence that the claim is actually misleading or false.
general_skills.when_to_apply: When the post package is incomplete or only loosely connected to the claim.

general_skills.title: Use broader knowledge for public events
general_skills.principle: For historical, military, political, scientific, or otherwise public-event claims, use general world knowledge and plausibility reasoning in addition to the provided post inputs.
general_skills.when_to_apply: When the claim concerns older events or facts that may be known beyond the post itself.

general_skills.title: Web search for public corroboration
general_skills.principle: When the local post package is insufficient for a historical or public-event claim, use a focused DuckDuckGo search to look for corroboration from reporting, institutions, or archival sources before treating the claim as false.
general_skills.when_to_apply: When the claim is about a public event and the post package alone cannot settle whether it is likely real or fake.

manipulated_media.title: Look for impossible visual claims
manipulated_media.principle: Check whether text overlays or narration make absolute medical, political, or scientific claims unsupported by the frame context.
manipulated_media.when_to_apply: When dramatic certainty appears in captions or audio.

misleading_caption.title: Separate caption from reality
misleading_caption.principle: Treat the caption as the main claim being judged, not as proof that the claim is true.
misleading_caption.when_to_apply: When the post caption makes a strong assertion.

misleading_caption.title: On-topic visuals are not proof
misleading_caption.principle: Visuals that merely depict the same subject as the caption do not verify that the claimed real-world event actually happened.
misleading_caption.when_to_apply: When the frames are thematically related to the claim but do not show documentary evidence.

misleading_caption.title: Extraordinary claims need documentary support
misleading_caption.principle: Extraordinary real-world claims should be treated as misleading when the post relies on stylized, composite, generic, or ambiguous imagery instead of credible documentary support.
misleading_caption.when_to_apply: When the caption makes an extraordinary claim and the visuals look illustrative, edited, or non-documentary.

out_of_context.title: Verify time cues
out_of_context.principle: Check transcript, OCR, and attached frames for timestamps, archive labels, or signs that the video predates the claimed event.
out_of_context.when_to_apply: When the post says the clip is recent or tied to a current event.

source_credibility.title: Inspect visible source signals
source_credibility.principle: Use source claims that are explicitly visible in the post text, transcript, OCR, or frames rather than hidden metadata shortcuts.
source_credibility.when_to_apply: When authenticity depends on who the post claims is speaking or publishing.

source_credibility.title: Public-event plausibility matters
source_credibility.principle: For claims about governments, militaries, public institutions, or widely reported events, do not assume falsehood merely because the post itself is not self-verifying; weigh background plausibility and prior knowledge as well.
source_credibility.when_to_apply: When the claim concerns state actors, historical incidents, or public events that may be known outside the post.

source_credibility.title: DuckDuckGo corroboration pass
source_credibility.principle: Run a targeted DuckDuckGo search for the central claim, named entities, dates, and organizations to check whether public reporting or official statements support the event.
source_credibility.when_to_apply: When a claim names organizations, dates, platforms, or public incidents that should leave a searchable trace.

temporal_inconsistency.title: Resolve timeline conflicts
temporal_inconsistency.principle: A mismatch between claimed date and visible/archive date is strong evidence of misinformation.
temporal_inconsistency.when_to_apply: When time references appear in the post or frames.

common_mistakes.description: Calling a post fake just because it sounds dramatic.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The model reacts to suspicious tone instead of the overall misleading factual impression.
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Judge whether the post actually presents a concrete misleading claim as true.
Down-rank unsupported absolute claims unless the evidence package substantiates them.

common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Temporal clues are often small and easy to miss.
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Inspect OCR and attached frames before issuing a verdict.

common_mistakes.description: Confusing missing in-package proof with falsehood.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The model over-relies on the local post package and treats incomplete evidence as proof the claim is fake.
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Use broader world knowledge and ask whether the claim is actually misleading, not just under-evidenced by this single post.

common_mistakes.description: Skipping a web corroboration check when the claim is about a searchable public event.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The system either trusts the post package too much or rejects the claim too quickly without looking for obvious public traces.
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Use a targeted DuckDuckGo search for public-event, historical, military, or institutional claims when local evidence is insufficient.
